hub_subtitle: Inglês para Viagens & Cotidiano com Foco em Listening

lesson.menu_desc: Compare your transport options in New York with comparatives and superlatives -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- Getting Around the City
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 14 -- Getting Around the City

listenings.text: Good morning, and here is your nine o'clock traffic and transit update for New York City. It is rush hour, and things are busier than usual. On the roads, downtown is more crowded than uptown right now, and there is heavy gridlock near the bridges, so a taxi is the slowest option this morning. If you are heading south, the subway is by far the fastest and most reliable way to travel. Trains are running on time, and the express is quicker than the local, as always. For anyone near the water, the ferry is the most scenic route, and it is far less crowded than the trains today. A quick tip: the toll on the tunnel is higher than the bridge this week, so if you are driving, the bridge is the cheaper choice. Whatever you choose, leave a little earlier than usual. Have a smooth trip.

Here is a short tip for comparing your options when you travel. When you compare two things, use a comparative. For short words, add e-r and than, like the subway is faster than the bus, or the walk is longer than the ride. For longer words, use more or less before the word, like a taxi is more expensive than the train, and the ferry is less crowded than the subway. When you compare three or more things and pick the top one, use a superlative with the. For short words, add e-s-t, like the subway is the fastest, and for longer words, use the most or the least, like the ferry is the most scenic. And two quick ones you just have to remember: good becomes better and the best, and bad becomes worse and the worst. Get these right, and you will always choose the smartest way to get around.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
a fee you pay to use a road, bridge, or tunnel
b
a traffic jam so bad that cars cannot move
c
a quicker route than the usual one
d
a circular junction where traffic flows around a center
e
a street where cars may drive in only one direction
f
a boat that carries people across water on a set route
g
a schedule showing when buses or trains run
h
full of people, with very little space
i
easy to use and saving you time or effort
j
working well and unlikely to fail or be late
k
offering beautiful views along the way
l
to take someone to a place and leave them there

paras: Ask ten New Yorkers for the best way to cross Manhattan, and you will get ten different answers. The truth is that no single option wins every time. The right choice depends on the hour, the weather, and how much you want to spend. What every local agrees on is this: the smartest traveler compares the options before stepping outside.

The subway is usually the fastest and most reliable way to move at rush hour, when the roads are far more crowded than the tracks. A taxi feels more comfortable, but in heavy gridlock it is often slower and more expensive than the train, and the toll on a tunnel can make it pricier still. A rideshare is more convenient for luggage, though it will drop you off in the same traffic. Walking is the cheapest choice of all, and for short trips it is sometimes quicker than waiting for a train.

Then there is the option most visitors forget: the ferry. It is not the fastest way to travel, but it is by far the most scenic, and it is usually less crowded than the subway. On a clear morning, the ride past the skyline is the best part of the day. So before you choose, ask yourself one question: today, do I want the fastest route, the cheapest, or the most beautiful? In this city, the right answer changes every single day.
reading.source: Adapted from a New York City visitor transportation guide

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The subway is always the best way to travel in New York.
No single option always wins, so the smartest traveler compares the choices before deciding.
Visitors should never take the ferry because it is too slow.

items: The text says one transport option is always the best in New York.
It says no single option wins every time; the right choice depends on the hour, weather, and budget.
At rush hour, the roads are more crowded than the subway tracks.
The second paragraph says the roads are far more crowded than the tracks at rush hour.
According to the text, walking is the most expensive option.
It says walking is the cheapest choice of all, and sometimes quicker than waiting for a train.
The ferry is described as the most scenic way to travel.
The last paragraph says the ferry is by far the most scenic and usually less crowded than the subway.

than the train. The ferry is not the fastest, but it is the most

gapfill.text: Comparatives compare TWO things: short words add -er + than (faster than), long words use more/less + than (more expensive than). Superlatives pick the top of THREE or more: use the + -est (the fastest) or the + most/least (the most scenic). Irregular: good - better - the best; bad - worse - the worst.

bank.label: Useful language for comparing transport options
items: The subway is faster than the bus.
A taxi is more expensive than the train.
The ferry is the most scenic way to travel.
Walking is the cheapest option of all.
The express is quicker than the local.
This route is less crowded than the other one.
The subway is by far the most reliable choice.
For me, the train is more convenient than a rideshare.

lf.text: Two things = comparative (-er + than for short words, more/less + than for long words). Three or more, pick the top = superlative (the + -est, or the most/least). Remember the irregulars: good - better - best; bad - worse - worst.

items.situation: Compare the subway and a taxi at rush hour.
tips: 'The subway is faster than a taxi in gridlock.'
Add why: the roads are more crowded.

items.situation: A visitor asks for the most beautiful way to cross the river.
tips: 'The ferry is the most scenic route.'
Add that it is less crowded too.

items.situation: Say which option is the cheapest for a short trip.
Compare the express train and the local train.
tips: 'The express is quicker than the local.'
Name the trade-off if you can.

items.situation: Sum up the best way to get around New York in one line.
tips: Use a superlative for your top pick.
'The subway is the most reliable way to travel.'

items: Compare the subway and a taxi for a rush-hour trip. Use a comparative with 'than'.
Recommend the most scenic way to see the city. Use a superlative with 'the most'.
A friend has heavy luggage. Compare a rideshare and the subway and advise the more convenient one.
Sum up your top choice for getting around New York in one sentence, using a superlative.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = fastest, 2 = crowded, 3 = expensive, 4 = scenic, 5 = cheapest.
Comparative (two things): 'The subway is faster than the bus.' / 'A taxi is more expensive than the train.'
Superlative (top of three or more): 'The ferry is the most scenic.' / 'Walking is the cheapest.'
Irregular forms: good - better - the best; bad - worse - the worst.
answerkey.note: Comparative = compare two (-er + than, or more/less + than). Superlative = pick the top of three or more (the + -est, or the most/least).
